turns.content: I got this error when running my cargo check: error: failed to parse manifest at `./Cargo.toml`: the specified package cannot be found. I thought if I just delete the lock file and let cargo regenerate it, that should fix everything since the dependencies are in the workspace.

Deleting a Cargo.lock file is a common first step when you add new crates to your project, but relying on it for reproducibility is a mistake. The real issue here isn't just about regenerating the lock; it's about understanding how cargo handles dependency resolution and version pinning. Your assumption that deleting the lock will magically solve an existing configuration error ignores the fact that cargo uses the lock file to ensure every build matches exactly what was defined in your Cargo.toml. When you regenerate a lock, you are not just fixing a missing file; you are enforcing deterministic builds so that different developers or CI environments produce identical artifacts without manual intervention.

That makes sense for consistency, but I'm still confused about why the library crates themselves don't include their own Cargo.lock files when they publish on crates.io. If we want reproducibility, shouldn't everything be locked at every step?

The reason crate authors do not ship a Cargo.lock with their binaries is that it would break the build process for anyone using them who has different local versions of transitive dependencies. A library's binary only needs to contain its own direct dependencies; including a lock file would force downstream users to match exactly those specific dependency versions even if they want to upgrade unrelated parts of their own tree. The standard best practice is to let the consuming application manage its own Cargo.lock to ensure that when you commit your project, it captures exactly which versions were resolved for your build environment without constraining other projects that depend on your library.
